q_44809.question_text: Will the USDA-posted recall by Reser’s Fine Foods, Inc. of its Ready-To-Eat Pasta Salad Product issued June 25, 2026 be closed before September 1, 2026?
q_44809.reasoning:
(a) **Time left until resolution**
Today is July 24, 2026. The resolution date is September 1, 2026. This gives **39 days** for the recall to be closed.

(b) **Status quo outcome if nothing changes**
The recall was issued on June 25, 2026, and as of the research date, remains Active. If the recall status doesn't change, it resolves as NO.

(c) **Scenario for NO outcome**
The recall remains Active beyond September 1, 2026. This could occur if:
- The recall process takes longer than 39 days (standard for many recalls)
- Effectiveness checks are still pending or incomplete
- Retail distribution verification is ongoing
- No clear resolution pathway has been established yet

(d) **Scenario for YES outcome**
The FSIS changes the recall status from Active to Closed before September 1, 2026. This would require:
- Rapid completion of effectiveness checks (checking that recalled product has been removed from distribution)
- Swift verification that all distributed products have been recovered or properly handled
- Quick confirmation of distribution lists and retail compliance

(e) **Base rate — how often do similar recalls close within ~6 weeks**
No specific base rate data is provided in the Background, Research, or Resolution criteria. The research explicitly states: "No information on the closure status of the recall by a specific date is available."

(f) **How the live research moves me from base rate**
The research provides several relevant details:
- This is a relatively small recall (~5,300 pounds)
- The product has a short shelf life (USE BY JUL/16/26) — meaning most product would naturally expire/be discarded before September 1
- The issue was self-reported by Reser's (suggesting cooperation)
- No confirmed adverse reactions reported yet
- Distribution was to foodservice locations in 7 states (not nationwide)
- The FSIS indicated it would conduct effectiveness checks and post retail distribution lists "when available"

These factors suggest *some* path to closure, but the phrase "when available" suggests the process may still be in early stages. The expiration date on product (July 16, 2026) is a major factor — by September 1, the product would be well past its use-by date, potentially simplifying closure. However, the research shows no actual progress reports on effectiveness checks or closure timeline as of the research date.

(g) **Community prediction**
No community prediction is provided in the question.

---

**REASONING:**

The key insight is the product's short shelf life. The recalled product bore a "USE BY JUL/16/26" date. By September 1, 2026, this product will be **47 days past** its use-by date. This means:
- Consumers would naturally discard it
- Retailers would remove it from shelves
- Foodservice locations would have disposed of it

This dramatically increases the likelihood that a recall can be closed, as the natural product lifecycle eliminates the contaminated inventory without requiring extensive recovery efforts.

However, counterbalancing factors:
- The FSIS still needs to conduct and complete effectiveness checks
- Retail distribution lists still need to be posted
- The research notes "No information on the closure status of the recall by a specific date is available," suggesting no closure timeline has been announced
- Bureaucratic processes can move slowly

The timeframe is 39 days — moderately tight but plausible given:
- Small volume (5,300 lbs)
- Limited distribution (7 states, foodservice only)
- Product naturally expiring during the recall window
- Company cooperation (self-reported)

Food Safety News recalls and FSIS closure patterns suggest small, limited-distribution recalls with clear product expiration dates can close within 6-8 weeks when effectiveness checks are straightforward. This recall appears to fit that profile, but we have no explicit confirmation that closure procedures have begun or what the timeline expectations are.

Balancing the natural product expiration (a major YES factor) against the lack of announced closure timeline and typical bureaucratic timelines (NO factors), I estimate this at slightly better than even odds, but not strongly.

**Probability: 62%**
q_44809.research_text:
On June 25, 2026, Reser's Fine Foods, Inc. of Halifax, N.C., recalled approximately 5,300 pounds of "Molly's Kitchen California Style Pasta Salad" due to misbranding and undeclared allergens [fsis.usda.gov](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/recalls-alerts/resers-fine-foods-inc--recalls-ready-eat-pasta-salad-product-due-misbranding-and), [foodsafetynews.com](https://www.foodsafetynews.com/2026/06/resers-recalls-mollys-kitchen-pasta-salad/). The product, produced on June 11, 2026, and bearing a "USE BY JUL/16/26 430" date and establishment number "P-00874," may contain chicken salad with undeclared egg and milk [fsis.usda.gov](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/recalls-alerts/resers-fine-foods-inc--recalls-ready-eat-pasta-salad-product-due-misbranding-and), [wgcu.org](https://www.wgcu.org/health/2026-06-25/resers-fine-foods-recalls-ready-to-eat-pasta-salad-product-due-to-misbranding-undeclared-allergens/).

The recalled product was shipped to distributors in Alabama, Florida, North Carolina, New Jersey, South Carolina, Tennessee, and Virginia for further distribution to foodservice locations [fsis.usda.gov](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/recalls-alerts/resers-fine-foods-inc--recalls-ready-eat-pasta-salad-product-due-due-misbranding-and), [nj.com](https://www.nj.com/food/2026/06/popular-ready-to-eat-pasta-salad-recalled-due-to-misbranding-undeclared-allergens.html). The issue was discovered when Reser's Fine Foods notified the FSIS of the mislabeling [fsis.usda.gov](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/recalls-alerts/resers-fine-foods-inc--recalls-ready-eat-pasta-salad-product-due-misbranding-and). As of June 25, 2026, there have been no confirmed reports of adverse reactions [fsis.usda.gov](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/recalls-alerts/resers-fine-foods-inc--recalls-ready-eat-pasta-salad-product-due-misbranding-and). Consumers are advised to discard the product or return it [fsis.usda.gov](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/recalls-alerts/resers-fine-foods-inc--recalls-ready-eat-pasta-salad-product-due-misbranding-and). The FSIS is conducting effectiveness checks and will post retail distribution lists when available [fsis.usda.gov](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/recalls-alerts/resers-fine-foods-inc--recalls-ready-eat-pasta-salad-product-due-misbranding-and). No information on the closure status of the recall by a specific date is available.
